Despite their immense potential, the millet value chain in India faces several bottlenecks including limited market access, lack of visibility for small-scale producers, weak integration between stakeholders, and inadequate consumer awareness. Farmers and Self-Help Groups (SHGs) often struggle to access fair markets, while startups and processors find it difficult to source quality produce directly. On the other side, consumers have limited platforms to explore and purchase diverse millet-based products.Detailed DescriptionThere is a pressing need for a digital platform that bridges these gaps by integrating all key stakeholders of the millet and pulses value chain. The proposed solution should create a digital marketplace that enables farmers, Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), SHGS, startups, processors, and consumers to interact on a common platform. Such a system should provide functionalities for online trading, e-commerce integration, quality certification, traceability of produce, logistics support, and payment facilitation. The platform should also promote millet-based value-added products, nutritional awareness, and branding initiatives. Additionally, it should be multilingual, user-friendly, and accessible even in rural areas with limited internet connectivity.Expected SolutionThe expected solution is a robust, scalable, and secure digital marketplace platform for millets. It should:Link farmers and SHGs directly with buyers, processors, and consumers, ensuring fair pricing.Facilitate value chain integration including procurement, quality assurance, logistics, and payment gateways.Promote branding and marketing of millet-based products under ""Shree Anna.""Provide traceability of produce from farm to fork to build consumer trust.Be available in regional languages with simple navigation for rural users.Integrate features for government schemes, subsidies, and certifications to support millet entrepreneurship.

data.title: Low-Cost machinery for picking of cotton.
data.theme: Low-Cost machinery for picking of cotton.
data.full_description: BackgroundCotton cultivation in India is predominantly carried out by small and marginal farmers, who constitute over 70% of the farming population, Manual cotton picking is labour-intensive, time-consuming, and increasingly challenged by labour shortages and rising costs. Mechanized solutions available in the market are often unaffordable and unsuitable for small landholdings.DescriptionDesign and develop a low-cost, portable, and efficient cotton-picking machine tailored for small and marginal farmers. The machine should:Operate effectively in fragmented and uneven cotton fields.Be lightweight and easy to handle by a single user.Minimize damage to cotton bolls and plants.Be powered by battery or solar energy for rural deployment.Picked Cotton with minimum trash content.Proposed InnovationA compact, semi-automated cotton-picking device equipped with:Al-powered vision system (using Open CV and Tensor Flow) to detect mature cotton bolls.Storage compartment for collected cotton.Modular design for easy maintenance and scalability.BenefitsReduces dependency on manual labour.Enhances picking efficiency and speed.Improves cotton quality by minimizing contamination.Empowers small farmers with affordable mechanization.Implementation ScopeThis solution can be piloted in cotton-growing regions of the country. It aligns with the goals of Atmanirbhar Bharat and Digital India by promoting indigenous, tech-enabled agricultural tools.

data.title: Designing/developing more efficient and suitable jute ribboner Machine to extract bark/fibre for enhancing in-situ retting technology of jute.
data.theme: Designing/developing more efficient and suitable jute ribboner Machine to extract bark/fibre for enhancing in-situ retting technology of jute.
data.full_description: BackgroundJute is the second most crucial fibre crop in India after cotton, known for its diverse applications and economic significance. Widely used in manufacturing products like gunny bags, ropes, carpets, jute diversified products etc. It is valued for its low cost, colour, lustre and strength. The extraction of jute fibre is known as jute retting. The traditional approach to jute retting involves harvesting of 100 to 120 days mature jute plants, bundling them, and immersing the bundles in water bodies such as rivers or ponds. Traditional water retting is very popular in our country however, retting of whole jute crop requires large quantity of clean water. Due to weather vagaries in recent years, jute retting has been badly affected by scarcity of water during the harvesting time i.e., July August months in the jute growing belts of India (mainly in Assam, West Bengal, Bihar, Meghalaya, Odisha, Nagaland, Tripura). The condition has forced the farmers to use the stagnant and insufficient water over and over again for retting, which ultimately leads to poor quality of fibre and economic loss for the farmers.DescriptionMany research institutes like ICAR-CRIJAF, ICAR-NINFET etc. have developed a new method of retting known as ribbon retting Prior to retting, sticks are separated and the fiber-containing bark part of the jute stems is allowed to ret underwater, which requires very less amount of water and reduces the regular retting time by 4 to 5 days (generally traditional retting takes time of about 20 to 25 days). Thus, the process takes around 15 to 18 days to complete the retting.The institutes have developed power-operated Jute Ribboner machine for the extraction of jute ribbons from freshly harvested jute plants without breaking sticks. It is powered by a single phase 3 hp electric motor and can be operated by a 5 hp Diesel engine with slight modification. It consists of two sets of stick breaker-cum-ribbon pulling units placed one above the other. Five to six jute plants can be fed in one set of breaker units, there by 10-12 plants can be extracted at a time by two units. The principle involves feeding jute plants by the butt end in between the upper breaking rollers, which crushes the stick about 5 cm, and then pulling off the ribbon by the lower rollers at high speed and placing the ribbon on the conveyor belt for delivery at front side of the machine. The sticks of the feeding plants were ejected intact from the upper tray of the machine. The existing machines involves two-way operation for complete ribbon extraction.Another power operated portable machine named ""Bast Fibre Extractor was developed by ICAR-CRIJAF, Barrackpore to extract green ribbon from jute, mesta, sunnhemp and ramie plants by breaking the stick into small pieces. The machine works on the principle of beating the canes progressively down its length and scrapping when the operator pulls out the canes. It needs two workers for operation, one for feeding the canes and its ribbon separation in the machine and the other for helping in relay of the canes and removing wastes. The machine salvages ribbon from thin plants (basal diameter less than 0.5 cm), which is normally discarded under conventional method. Such thin plants constitute about 15% by weight of total harvested biomass, which is added to the total fibre yield.Even after such efforts from the researches by developing new methods of ribbon retting in small volume of water and getting better quality of fibre, some issues like, manual feeding of jute plants in the ribboner, low extraction efficiency, breaking of inner jute sticks, less transportability etc., have left the machines less appreciated and adaptability is very poor among the farmers. They require high capacity and cost-effective ribboner machines capable of ribboning more quantity of jute plants in short period of time and easy to carry near to the jute fields so that they can easily carry the jute bark/ribbon to nearby small ponds or waterbodies or in-situ excavated plots inside jute fields for retting.Expected SolutionThe efficiency of the ribboning machines needs to be enhanced to extract ribbons from high volume of green plants in a short period of time to make them more cost effective and needs better portability. The machine should be operated by different power sources An electric motor or engine can be fitted on the frame for its operation. Power tiller or small tractor can also be used with it for ease of transport.

data.title: Early Detection of Wheat Diseases
data.theme: Early Detection of Wheat Diseases
data.full_description: DescriptionWheat is affected by several diseases, primarily fungal diseases like rusts, smuts, powdery mildew, and root and head blights, but also bacterial and viral diseases such as Black Chaff and Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus. These pathogens can cause symptoms ranging from leaf spots and wilting to blighting of the ears and grain, leading to significant yield losses. Major wheat diseases such as rusts, blight, and Karnal bunt often spread rapidly, leading to significant yield loss.Expected SolutionDevelopment of Al/ML-based image recognition tools integrated with drone/satellite data and mobile apps to provide real-time detection, mapping, and alerts to farmers.

data.title: National Campaign and Digital Platform for Reducing Edible Oil Consumption
data.theme: National Campaign and Digital Platform for Reducing Edible Oil Consumption
data.full_description: BackgroundIndia stands at a critical health and economic juncture due to excessive edible oil consumption. Per capita consumption of edible oil in India is approximately 19.3 kg per year, nearly 60% higher than the ICMR-recommended level of 12 kg. This overconsumption contributes to rising obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular diseases, imposing a significant economic burden projected at USD 4.58 trillion by 2030 due to non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and associated productivity losses.On the economic front, India consumed 27.8 million tonnes of edible oil in 2023-24, of which over 56% was imported, draining substantial amount in foreign exchange annually. This dependency on imports not only exposes the country to global price volatility but also disincentivizes domestic oilseed cultivation, undermining farmer incomes and the goal of Atmanirbhar Bharat. In this context, the Hon’ble Prime Minister, during the 119th episode of Mann Ki Baat (Feb 2025), called for a 10% reduction in edible oil consumption, positioning it as a strategic, economic, and health intervention.Detailed DescriptionThe challenge is to develop a comprehensive digital platform supported by multi-channel outreach to nudge consumer behavior toward reduced oil consumption, while promoting healthier alternatives and domestic oils. The platform should:Create awareness through culturally contextualized campaigns, visual cues, and personalized recommendations.Provide oil consumption tracking tools integrated with health metrics to make users aware of the health impact of excess oil intake.Feature AI-driven personalized diet suggestions, focusing on low-oil recipes, regional cuisines, and nutrition-based meal planning.Include e-learning modules for schools and communities on balanced diets and healthy cooking practices.Enable collaboration with restaurants and food manufacturers to highlight low-oil options through digital labeling and certification.Integrate policy nudges, such as differentiated GST for healthier oils and reward systems for compliance, creating positive reinforcement loops.Expected SolutionA mobile-first, multilingual platform supported by behavioral nudges and IoT integration for household consumption tracking. The solution should include:AI-powered consumption audit tools with health risk indicators.Behavior changes features and rewards (reward points, social sharing for healthy habits).Integration with food delivery apps and retail chains to promote low-oil certified products and recipes.Real-time dashboards for policymakers to track consumption patterns and campaign impact at district/state levels.Partnership APIs for food service providers, enabling digital labels on menus showing oil content and healthy alternatives.Blockchain-based transparency system for validating certification and claims from restaurants and packaged food manufacturers.The campaign should target schools, households, and institutional kitchens, including Mid-Day Meal (MDM) schemes, hospitals, and canteens, to create large-scale impact.

data.title: AI-Enabled Value Chain Platform for Oilseed Self-Reliance
data.theme: AI-Enabled Value Chain Platform for Oilseed Self-Reliance
data.full_description: BackgroundDespite significant growth in oilseed production, India remains heavily dependent on imports for 55-60% of its edible oil needs, draining foreign exchange and exposing farmers to global market volatility. While production increased from 27.51 million tonnes in 2014-15 to 42.61 million tonnes in 2024-25, the domestic edible oil demand of 27.8 million tonnes in 2023-24 still requires imports worth over ₹1 lakh crore.The government launched the National Mission on Edible Oils–Oil Palm (NMEO-OP) in 2021 and announced a new oilseeds mission to double production to 69 million tonnes by 2030-31. However, achieving this ambitious goal requires better integration across the value chain, from seed to shelf.Detailed DescriptionThe objective is to develop an AI-enabled value chain platform to ensure seamless coordination among stakeholders—farmers, FPOs, processors, and retailers—while optimizing logistics and minimizing post-harvest losses. The platform should:Integrate predictive analytics for demand-supply trends and price forecasting.Provide advisories for crop planning, weather alerts, and pest management using AI and satellite data.Offer real-time visibility into procurement, storage, and processing infrastructure for efficient utilization.Enable direct farmer-to-market linkages.Include a credit and insurance facilitation module linked with performance-based incentives.Expected SolutionA unified digital platform featuring:AI-powered decision-support dashboards for policymakers and stakeholders.Blockchain-based traceability of oilseed produce from farm to fork.Integrated logistics management and warehouse mapping for reducing losses.Integration with Agri-Stack and national digital agriculture initiatives.

data.title: AI-Powered Import Impact Simulator for Palm Oil Tariffs
data.theme: AI-Powered Import Impact Simulator for Palm Oil Tariffs
data.full_description: BackgroundIndia's high dependence on palm oil imports, primarily Crude Palm Oil (CPO), exposes the edible oil sector to global price volatility and market shocks. Customs duties on CPO, decided through inter-ministerial consultations, play a crucial role in balancing farmer incomes with consumer affordability. However, frequent adjustments disrupt domestic markets and farmer confidence under the NMEO-OP framework, impacting long-term self-reliance goals.Detailed DescriptionDevelop a simulation tool capable of modeling the impact of varying customs duties on CPO imports. The system should analyze historical trade data, global price indices, and domestic production gaps to predict impacts on farmer prices, cultivation trends, and consumer costs. The tool should also incorporate real-time data from global markets and NMEO-OP progress to ensure evidence-based policy formulation.Expected SolutionA web-based AI-driven simulator that uses machine learning to forecast outcomes of tariff adjustments under multiple scenarios. Features should include real-time dashboards for policymakers, visual analytics for import dependency, and predictive tools for planning interventions. Integration with global commodity data APIs and dynamic scenario modeling will enhance decision-making for self-reliance in palm oil production.

data.title: AI-Driven Yield Optimization Platform for Oilseed Crops
data.theme: AI-Driven Yield Optimization Platform for Oilseed Crops
data.full_description: BackgroundIndia's oilseed yields remain significantly lower than those of global leaders like the USA and Brazil. Nearly 72% of oilseed cultivation is rainfed, with limited adoption of advanced agronomic practices. This yield gap perpetuates heavy import dependency (over 56%) for edible oils, costing India over $16 billion annually. To reduce vulnerability to global shocks and ensure Atmanirbhar Bharat, technology-driven solutions are required to close the productivity gap.Detailed DescriptionDevelop an AI-enabled platform that leverages big data, weather forecasts, soil health information, and historical yield patterns to provide personalized advisories for farmers. The platform should predict yield potential, identify gaps, and recommend interventions such as irrigation strategies, pest management, and adoption of high-yielding seed varieties. Real-time alerts to prevent shifts to competing crops are essential to stabilize oilseed acreage.Expected SolutionA mobile and web-based application that uses machine learning and remote sensing (satellite imagery, IoT sensors) to deliver dynamic yield forecasts and precision advisory services. The solution should include benchmarking against global yield standards, simulation of scenarios for farmer decision-making, and multilingual accessibility to ensure large-scale adoption by smallholders.

data.title: Crop Shift Mitigation App for Promoting Oilseed Farming
data.theme: Crop Shift Mitigation App for Promoting Oilseed Farming
data.full_description: BackgroundFarmers are increasingly shifting from oilseeds to crops like paddy, sugarcane, and maize, attracted by assured procurement and price stability. Oilseeds are often relegated to marginal, rainfed areas, limiting expansion and perpetuating dependence on imports. This trend threatens the sustainability of India’s edible oil self-sufficiency roadmap.Detailed DescriptionThe challenge is to develop a digital platform that uses predictive analytics and market intelligence to make oilseed cultivation more attractive. The system should provide comparative crop economics, highlight long-term profitability, and integrate access to government support schemes like NMEO-OS. It should also address risk factors such as weak procurement systems by offering assurance tools and linkages with Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs).Expected SolutionAn application offering real-time price alerts, virtual profitability simulations, and access to schemes and subsidies. It should feature crop economics dashboards, weather-based advisories, and market linkages to enable informed decision-making. Gamification and incentive-based features can encourage farmers to maintain or expand oilseed acreage.

data.title: Hedging Platform for Oilseed Price Risk Management
data.theme: Hedging Platform for Oilseed Price Risk Management
data.full_description: BackgroundOilseed farmers in India face acute price volatility without adequate hedging tools. Suspension of commodity trading platforms has worsened their exposure to unpredictable market fluctuations, leading to distress sales and income instability. In contrast, developed markets like the US and Brazil use futures and options to mitigate such risks.Detailed DescriptionDesign a digital platform that allows virtual hedging and risk management for oilseed farmers. The system should integrate price forecasting models, forward contracts, and transparent trading systems accessible to FPOs and smallholders. Blockchain integration can ensure transaction security and trust, while AI algorithms provide predictive analytics for market trends.Expected SolutionA blockchain-based mobile application featuring simulated futures trading, AI-driven price prediction, and e-contracts for forward sales. Additional components should include real-time market alerts, NCDEX-style integration, and educational modules to improve financial literacy among farmers regarding risk management practices.

data.title: Value Chain Integration System for Oilseed By-Products
data.theme: Value Chain Integration System for Oilseed By-Products
data.full_description: BackgroundFarmers often fail to capture value from oilseed by-products like soymeal due to market asymmetries and volatility. Events like the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict caused price distortions, where consumer prices surged while farm-gate prices stagnated. The lack of structured market linkages for by-products reduces farmer profitability and discourages investment in oilseed cultivation.Detailed DescriptionCreate a digital value chain platform connecting farmers, processors, and buyers for oilseed by-products. The system should provide real-time demand-supply visibility, enable direct transactions, and forecast market trends for by-products such as meal, cakes, and husk. It should also identify export opportunities for surplus and specialty by-products.Expected SolutionAn IoT-integrated marketplace application featuring smart contracts for transaction security, predictive analytics for price volatility, and tools for global export matchmaking. By leveraging blockchain and AI, the solution will empower farmers to monetize every part of the value chain effectively.

data.title: Digital Decision-Support and Incentive Platform for Oil Palm Farmers
data.theme: Digital Decision-Support and Incentive Platform for Oil Palm Farmers
data.full_description: BackgroundIndia’s efforts to expand oil palm cultivation under NMEO-OP face hesitation from marginal and small farmers, who constitute the majority of cultivators. These farmers are hesitant due to the long gestation period of 4–5 years before returns begin, misconceptions about oil palm causing deforestation, and uncertainty about assured income. Weak access to credible information, financing, and localized advisory services further reduces adoption, keeping cultivation far below potential.Detailed DescriptionA key challenge is building trust and confidence among smallholders by providing reliable information, financial risk-sharing mechanisms, and transparent monitoring of benefits. Farmers require a digital ecosystem that combines knowledge dissemination, financial incentives, and market linkages. The system should address sustainability concerns through verified information, provide tailored advisory services to marginal farmers, and offer financial visibility during the gestation period. Such a solution would counter misinformation, reduce risk perception, and improve participation in oil palm cultivation.Expected SolutionA digital decision-support and incentive platform can play a transformative role in addressing the challenges faced by marginal and small oil palm farmers. It should integrate multilingual, mobile-based awareness and advisory modules to dispel environmental misconceptions with verified data, while also offering risk-sharing features such as crop insurance and micro-credit options to reduce farmer vulnerability during the long gestation period. A farmer profiling system would enable targeted interventions tailored to the needs of smallholders, and community dashboards showcasing success stories could build trust, inspire confidence, and encourage wider adoption of oil palm cultivation.
